points: Agriculture is a primary economic activity in India, engaging two-thirds of the population; farming types include primitive subsistence (slash and burn), intensive subsistence (high pressure on land), and commercial farming including plantations.

India has three distinct cropping seasons: Rabi (winter crops like wheat), Kharif (monsoon crops like paddy), and Zaid (summer crops like watermelon), ensuring year-round agricultural productivity based on climatic variations.
The degree of commercialisation in agriculture varies across regions, where a staple crop in one state (e.g., rice in Odisha) may be a commercial crop in another (e.g., Haryana and Punjab).

India is a leading producer of various food grains (rice, wheat, millets, pulses), beverage crops (tea, coffee), and horticulture crops (fruits and vegetables), with specific geographical requirements for each.

Non-food and industrial crops like rubber, cotton (grown in black soil), and jute (the 'Golden Fibre') are essential raw materials for the industrial sector and contribute significantly to experimental and export economies.
Technological diversity is reflected in crop production, from rain-fed millets with high nutritional value to capital-intensive plantations and irrigation-dependent cereals like rice and sugarcane.

Post-Independence reforms focused on institutional changes like land consolidation and abolition of Zamindari, followed by technological revolutions such as the Green and White Revolutions to boost productivity.

Modern farmer support involves technical and financial schemes including Kisan Credit Card (KCC), Personal Accident Insurance Scheme (PAIS), Minimum Support Price (MSP), and crop insurance against natural calamities.

The Bhoodan-Gramdan movement initiated by Vinoba Bhave, known as the 'Blood-less Revolution,' aimed at voluntary land redistribution to provide livelihoods for landless villagers through community cooperation.
